ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for moutabbal. FOODON_03311737 (eggplant food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific term for roasted eggplant and tahini dip is not present in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (processed aubergine/eggplant) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for moutabbal (roasted eggplant and tahini dip) exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for moutabbal as a specific food product. SNOMED CT does not catalogue this dish.

serving.description: 1 serving as a dip or mezze portion
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of moutabbal as a mezze dip is approximately 100 g, served with flatbread or crudités.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Baba Ghanoush (roasted eggplant and tahini dip); cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for eggplant and tahini components
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Moutabbal is closely related to baba ghanoush; both are roasted eggplant and tahini dips. USDA FoodData Central entry for baba ghanoush (FDC ID 173944) was used as the primary nutrient reference, with values adapted to reflect the typical moutabbal formulation (higher tahini proportion, addition of pomegranate seeds as garnish). Pomegranate seeds contribute negligible macronutrient change at garnish quantities. Values represent a composite estimate for a standard moutabbal preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of eggplant-based and legume-tahini dip foods; analogous estimate based on roasted eggplant (GI ~15) and tahini (GI ~10) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for moutabbal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 based on the dominant ingredients: roasted eggplant (GI approximately 15, low GI vegetable) and tahini (GI approximately 10, sesame paste with negligible glycemic impact). The composite GI is estimated at 15 (low). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (15 × 12.0) / 100 = 1.8.

hypertension.guidance: Moutabbal contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as salted flatbread or processed crackers.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and high dietary fibre (3.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Moutabbal has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 15 and provides 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it a suitable dip option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; the tahini component contributes healthy unsaturated fats that support glycemic stability.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High unsaturated fat content from tahini and olive oil; zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Moutabbal is rich in monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from tahini and olive oil (~8.3 g per 100 g combined), contains zero cholesterol, and provides plant sterols from sesame, making it a heart-friendly dip when consumed in appropriate portions as part of a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Moutabbal (also spelled mutabbal or mutabal) is a classic Levantine and Turkish mezze dip made primarily from fire-roasted or grilled eggplant (aubergine) blended with tahini (sesame paste), lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil, and typically garnished with pomegranate seeds, fresh parsley, and a drizzle of olive oil. The dish is closely related to baba ghanoush but is distinguished by its higher tahini content, which gives it a richer, nuttier, and creamier texture compared to the more smoky and lighter baba ghanoush. The eggplant is traditionally charred directly over an open flame or under a grill to impart a characteristic smoky flavour before the flesh is scooped out and blended. Moutabbal is a staple of Turkish and Lebanese cuisine and is widely served as part of a mezze spread alongside hummus, tabbouleh, and flatbread. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~152 kcal per 100 g), healthy unsaturated fats from tahini and olive oil, dietary fibre from eggplant (~3.2 g per 100 g), and a low glycemic index (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. The sesame content means it is a source of calcium, iron, and plant-based protein. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, though individuals with sesame allergy must avoid it. In Singapore, moutabbal is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants as part of a cold mezze selection.
common_user_queries: Is moutabbal the same as baba ghanoush?
What is the glycemic index of moutabbal?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for moutabbal exists in a primary regional approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central baba ghanoush entry (FDC ID 173944), which is the closest approved-source analogue. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database component data for roasted eggplant and tahini. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central baba ghanoush (FDC ID 173944) as the closest approved-source analogue for moutabbal. Moutabbal typically contains a higher proportion of tahini than baba ghanoush, which slightly increases fat and protein content; values have been adjusted accordingly. Pomegranate seeds are a garnish and contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical serving quantities. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based dip with no hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — entirely plant-based ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are reported from USDA SR Legacy sesame paste and eggplant component data. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous data for roasted eggplant and tahini; no direct GI measurement for moutabbal is available. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 12.0) / 100 = 1.8. FoodOn match is closest_parent (FOODON_03311737, eggplant food product); no specific moutabbal term exists. FoodEx2 match is closest_parent (A0FJP, processed aubergine); no specific code exists. SNOMED CT has no applicable concept. This dish is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine in Singapore (field survey source). The cuisine is classified as Turkish/Levantine reflecting the dish's dual Turkish and Levantine heritage.
